name: pwa-expert
description: Progressive Web App technology expert covering service workers, caching strategies, web app manifest, installation, offline functionality, and platform-specific PWA implementation

official_documentation: MDN Web Docs - Progressive Web Apps (https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Progressive_web_apps)

can_do: Implement service worker registration and lifecycle
Generate service worker code for all caching strategies
Create web app manifest JSON with all required and optional fields
Implement offline functionality and fallback pages
Configure background sync for deferred actions
Provide platform-specific PWA implementations (iOS, Android, Desktop)
Implement cache versioning and cleanup strategies
Debug service worker issues using Chrome DevTools
Create cache-first, network-first, and hybrid caching strategies
cannot_do: Make architectural decisions about when to use PWA vs native apps (opinion)
Recommend specific caching strategies without context (opinion)
Make subjective decisions about offline experience (opinion)

pwa_definition.statement: A Progressive Web App is a web application built using common web technologies (HTML, CSS, JavaScript) that works on any platform with a standards-compliant browser and provides app-like experiences including offline functionality, installation, and push notifications
pwa_definition.source: MDN Web Docs - Progressive Web Apps

pwa_characteristics.statement: PWAs must be: Progressive (work for all users), Responsive (all form factors), Connectivity-independent (offline/low quality networks), App-like (app-style interactions), Fresh (up-to-date via service workers), Safe (HTTPS), Discoverable (identifiable as applications), Re-engageable (push notifications), Installable (home screen), Linkable (shared via URL)
pwa_characteristics.source: Google Web.dev - What makes a good Progressive Web App

https_requirement.statement: PWAs must be served over HTTPS (except on localhost for development) because service workers have powerful capabilities that could be exploited if tampered with

sw_definition.statement: A service worker is a script that runs in the background, separate from a web page, enabling features that don't need a web page or user interaction. It acts as a programmable network proxy between the application and the network
sw_definition.source: MDN Web Docs - Service Worker API

sw_lifecycle.statement: Service worker lifecycle has 5 states: Registration (JavaScript registers the SW), Installation (SW downloaded and installed), Activation (SW becomes active and controls pages), Fetch/Message (SW handles events), Update (new SW installed when file changes)

sw_registration.statement: Register service workers after page load using navigator.serviceWorker.register() to avoid competing with initial page performance

sw_scope.statement: Service worker scope determines which pages it controls. Default scope is the location of the service worker file. Service worker can only control pages within its scope

install_event.statement: The install event fires once per service worker version. Use event.waitUntil() to extend the event until a Promise resolves. This is the ideal time to cache static assets

activate_event.statement: The activate event fires after installation. This is ideal for cache cleanup. Old service workers remain active until all tabs are closed unless skipWaiting() is called

fetch_event.statement: The fetch event is fired for all network requests from controlled pages. Use event.respondWith() to provide a custom response from cache or network

skipWaiting.statement: Call self.skipWaiting() in install event to skip the waiting state and activate immediately. Use with caution as it can break pages loaded with old service worker

clients_claim.statement: Call self.clients.claim() in activate event to take control of all pages immediately without requiring navigation

sw_update_detection.statement: Browser checks for service worker updates on navigation. If file changed (even 1 byte), downloads new version which installs in parallel with old SW and waits until all pages closed

cache_first.statement: Cache First strategy: Check cache first, return cached response if exists, otherwise fetch from network and cache for future use. Best for static assets (CSS, JS, images, fonts)
cache_first.example: async function cacheFirst(request) { const cached = await caches.match(request); return cached || fetch(request); }

network_first.statement: Network First strategy: Try network first, cache successful responses, fall back to cache if network fails. Best for dynamic content and API calls
network_first.example: async function networkFirst(request) { try { const response = await fetch(request); const cache = await caches.open('v1'); cache.put(request, response.clone()); return response; } catch { return caches.match(request); } }

stale_while_revalidate.statement: Stale While Revalidate strategy: Return cached version immediately, fetch new version in background to update cache. Best for content that changes occasionally but doesn't need immediate freshness

network_only.statement: Network Only strategy: Always fetch from network, no caching. Best for real-time critical data (stock prices, live scores)

cache_only.statement: Cache Only strategy: Only serve from cache, no network requests. Best for pre-cached app shell on repeat visits

cache_api.statement: Cache API provides caches.open(name), cache.add(), cache.addAll(), cache.put(), cache.delete(), caches.match(), caches.keys(), caches.delete() for managing cached responses

cache_versioning.statement: Implement cache versioning by including version in cache name (e.g., 'app-shell-v1'). On activate event, delete all caches except current version

manifest_definition.statement: Web app manifest is a JSON file that tells the browser about the PWA and how it should behave when installed on the user's device

manifest_required_fields.statement: Required manifest fields: name or short_name (app name), start_url (launch URL), display (display mode), icons (array with at least 192x192 and 512x512 PNG icons)

manifest_display_modes.statement: Manifest display modes: fullscreen (no browser UI), standalone (looks like standalone app - recommended), minimal-ui (standalone with minimal navigation), browser (normal browser tab)

manifest_icons.statement: Icons array requires objects with src (URL), sizes (e.g., '192x192'), type (e.g., 'image/png'), and purpose ('any', 'maskable', or 'monochrome'). Minimum required: 192x192 and 512x512

manifest_shortcuts.statement: Manifest shortcuts provide quick actions accessible from app icon context menu. Maximum 4 shortcuts (platform-dependent). Each shortcut needs name, url, and optional icons

manifest_scope.statement: Manifest scope defines navigation scope - URLs outside this scope open in browser. Service worker scope must contain manifest scope. Default is directory of manifest file

manifest_linking.statement: Link manifest in HTML head: <link rel="manifest" href="/manifest.json">

theme_color.statement: theme_color in manifest and <meta name="theme-color" content="#1890ff"> define browser UI color (address bar, status bar, task switcher)

installation_criteria.statement: For automatic installation prompt: Must be served over HTTPS, have registered service worker with fetch handler, have valid manifest (name, icons 192x192+512x512, start_url, display standalone/fullscreen/minimal-ui), user engagement (Chrome: 30+ seconds)

beforeinstallprompt_event.statement: beforeinstallprompt event fires when PWA installation criteria met. Call preventDefault() to defer prompt, store event, and show custom install UI later by calling prompt() on stored event

appinstalled_event.statement: appinstalled event fires when PWA is successfully installed. Use to hide install prompts and track installations

display_mode_detection.statement: Detect if running as installed PWA: window.matchMedia('(display-mode: standalone)').matches returns true when running standalone

offline_fallback.statement: Provide offline fallback page by precaching /offline.html during install and serving it for navigation requests when network fails

app_shell_architecture.statement: App Shell Architecture separates core application infrastructure (HTML, CSS, JS for UI - cached aggressively) from dynamic content (fetched and cached separately). App shell loads instantly on repeat visits

background_sync_api.statement: Background Sync API allows deferred actions when user has connectivity. Register sync: await self.registration.sync.register('sync-tag'). Handle: self.addEventListener('sync', event => { if (event.tag === 'sync-tag') event.waitUntil(doSync()); })
background_sync_api.source: MDN Web Docs - Background Sync API

background_sync_usage.statement: Background Sync use cases: Send messages when online, upload photos on WiFi, submit failed forms, sync data changes. Works even if app is closed

push_notifications_components.statement: Push Notifications require: Push Service (FCM for Chrome, APNs for Safari), Subscription (user permission + endpoint), Server (sends to endpoint), Service Worker (receives push events and shows notifications)

push_permission.statement: Request push permission: const permission = await Notification.requestPermission(). Returns 'granted', 'denied', or 'default'

workbox_definition.statement: Workbox is Google's production-ready service worker library that simplifies common service worker tasks with pre-built strategies, plugins, and utilities

workbox_strategies.statement: Workbox provides pre-built strategies: CacheFirst, NetworkFirst, StaleWhileRevalidate, NetworkOnly, CacheOnly as classes in workbox.strategies

workbox_routing.statement: Workbox routing: workbox.routing.registerRoute(urlPattern, strategy) registers routes with strategies. Supports RegExp, string, or function matchers

workbox_precaching.statement: Workbox precaching: workbox.precaching.precacheAndRoute(manifest) precaches assets with revision tracking. Manifest generated by build tools

ios_limitations.statement: iOS PWA limitations: No beforeinstallprompt event, no automatic install prompt, no push notifications (as of iOS 16), limited background sync, service worker limitations (no persistent background, 50MB cache quota)
ios_limitations.source: Apple WebKit - Service Workers on iOS

ios_meta_tags.statement: iOS-specific meta tags: <meta name="apple-mobile-web-app-capable" content="yes"> for standalone mode, <meta name="apple-mobile-web-app-status-bar-style" content="black-translucent"> for status bar, <link rel="apple-touch-icon" sizes="180x180" href="/icon.png"> for icon

android_advantages.statement: Android (Chrome) provides full PWA support: automatic install prompts, WebAPK generation (looks like native app), push notifications, background sync, file handling

desktop_pwa.statement: Desktop PWAs (Windows, Mac, Linux) install from address bar or menu, listed in Start Menu/Applications, run in separate window, support file handling and protocol handling

chrome_devtools.statement: Chrome DevTools Application tab provides: Service Workers panel (view, unregister, update on reload, bypass), Cache Storage panel (inspect cached resources), Clear Storage (remove all SW data)

lighthouse_pwa_audit.statement: Lighthouse PWA audit checks: Installability (installation criteria), PWA Optimized (performance, best practices), Icons (sizes and formats), Service Worker (registration), Manifest (structure). Target PWA score: 90+

sw_security.statement: Service worker security requirements: HTTPS required (prevents MITM), same-origin policy (can only control same-origin pages), no inline code (scripts must be external files), enforce Content Security Policy

cache_security.statement: Cache security best practices: Avoid caching PII unencrypted, store tokens in HTTP-only cookies (not cache), implement cache versioning, clear cache on logout

core_web_vitals.statement: PWAs improve Core Web Vitals: First Contentful Paint (app shell from cache), Time to Interactive (cached JS bundles), Largest Contentful Paint (cached images/assets), Cumulative Layout Shift (consistent cached layout)

cache_size_limits.statement: Implement cache size limits to prevent storage quota issues. Use expiration plugins (Workbox) or manual cleanup. Monitor cache size and remove old entries

register_after_load.statement: Register service worker after page load event to avoid competing with initial page performance

cache_selectively.statement: Cache selectively - don't cache everything. Only cache essential resources. Use cache size limits and cleanup strategies

version_caches.statement: Version cache names (e.g., 'app-v1', 'app-v2') to force updates. Delete old caches in activate event

respect_dismissals.statement: Respect install prompt dismissals - don't repeatedly prompt. Track dismissals in localStorage and wait for user engagement
